flashcards.front: What are the three main components of a computer system?
flashcards.back: The three main components are: (1) Central Processing Unit (CPU) - executes instructions, (2) Main Memory - stores data and instructions temporarily, (3) Input/Output devices - for user interaction and data exchange.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the Control Unit (CU) in CPU?
flashcards.back: The Control Unit directs the operation of the processor by interpreting instructions, controlling data flow between CPU components, and coordinating the execution of instructions.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)?
flashcards.back: The ALU performs arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, comparison) on binary data.

flashcards.front: What is the instruction execution cycle?
flashcards.back: The instruction execution cycle consists of: (1) Fetch - get instruction from memory, (2) Decode - interpret the instruction, (3) Execute - perform the operation, (4) Store - write result back to memory.

flashcards.front: What is an interrupt in computer system?
flashcards.back: An interrupt is a signal to the CPU indicating an event that requires immediate attention. It suspends the current execution, handles the event, and resumes normal operation.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between volatile and non-volatile memory?
flashcards.back: Volatile memory (like RAM) loses data when power is cut. Non-volatile memory (like ROM, HDD, SSD) retains data even without power.

flashcards.front: What is a system bus and what are its types?
flashcards.back: A system bus is a communication pathway connecting CPU, memory, and I/O devices. Types include: Data bus (transfers data), Address bus (specifies memory locations), and Control bus (carries control signals).
DMA allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention, improving system performance by freeing CPU for other tasks.
The bootstrap loader is a small program stored in ROM that initializes the system when powered on. It loads the operating system from storage into main memory.
Cache memory is a small, fast memory located between CPU and main memory. It stores frequently accessed data and instructions to reduce memory access time.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between RISC and CISC architectures?
flashcards.back: RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) has simple, few instructions executing in one cycle. CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) has many complex instructions that may take multiple cycles.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the system clock in a computer?
flashcards.back: The system clock generates timing pulses that synchronize all computer operations. It determines the speed at which instructions are executed.
I/O channels are specialized processors that handle input/output operations independently, allowing concurrent processing and reducing CPU overhead.
Memory hierarchy arranges storage in levels: Registers (fastest), Cache, Main Memory (RAM), and Secondary Storage (HDD, SSD) - balancing speed, size, and cost.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the operating system in hardware-software interaction?
flashcards.back: The operating system acts as an intermediary between hardware and applications, managing hardware resources and providing services for software execution.
